text: Authorize phase-two diligence. No term sheet yet. Four questions remain: entity mapping, OCPI governance, counterparty structure, and real liquidity.

This is a market-structure bet, not another AI app. If compute becomes financeable infrastructure, benchmark ownership matters. The company that owns the reference price and embeds it into institutional workflows could capture durable value.

There is enough public evidence to treat Ornn as more than a concept. The benchmark and data layer appear live. Bloomberg distribution is public. Hydra feeds infrastructure data from more than thirty thousand GPUs into Ornn analytics. Architect and Kalshi each add more visible market signal.
Ornn is trying to build three reinforcing layers: benchmark data, risk-transfer products, and distribution. If that pricing convention sticks, it can support underwriting and recurring value.

Legal ambiguity is still the biggest gating issue. Marketing suggests exchange positioning, while the terms narrow the service to reference use. The public record still does not resolve which products are live or under what regulatory basis.

If Ornn is taking principal exposure, this should be underwritten more like early market infrastructure than software. Public disclosures still leave open questions around collateral, capital, risk limits, and default management.

OCPI may be visible, but visibility is not underwritability. Public materials still do not prove methodology governance, contributor quality, data rights durability, or real market depth. Key trading metrics remain absent.

Move forward to gated phase-two diligence, not final approval. Advance only if management resolves legal structure, proves OCPI governance, demonstrates real liquidity, and tightens counterparty controls.
